   90秒蒙太奇，设置为休·杰克曼（Hugh Jackman）演唱彼得·艾伦（Peter Allen）的歌曲“我走之前，”是一个轻松愉快且充满笑声的回忆，展示了Trebek担任主持人36年以来不断变化的面貌，留着小胡子，没有胡须，留着黑发，留着灰色，穿了几十年来的西装。
  它庆祝了通常被海峡缠结的Trebek的恐怖时刻，向他展示了与参赛者的口头陪练和与之争夺手臂。
  ＆＃34;您真的让我感到不足，＆＃34;他告诉一名儿童选手。 ＆＃34;抱歉，＆＃34;她轻率地回答。
 特雷贝克（Trebek）在一个剪辑中穿着宽松的裤子行走，在另一个剪辑中打扮得像自由女神像，在另一个剪辑中穿着特洛伊士兵的服装。
 主持人声明“我不跳舞”的剪辑在他多年来跳舞的场景中显示。
 但是，致敬仍然令人悲伤和感伤，正如杰克曼（Jackman）唱的那样，当这么多声让你无法直言不讳时，很难说再见。 ＆＃34;和＆＃34;一次在我走之前，我想告诉你，我都会做一遍＆＃34; 
在最后的时刻，它显示了Trebek在数十年中的签字，并反复说：“这么久了，大家。”
 Trebek于11月8日去世，享年80岁，但他已经预先录制了几周的节目，并继续播出。被诊断出患有胰腺癌后，他继续工作了将近两年，自1984年起就一直呆在讲台上，一直待在讲台上。
 该节目将在下周继续进行，由老将＆＃34; Jeopardy！＆＃34;开始。冠军肯·詹宁斯。
 本周最后的Trebek插曲从星期一开始，主持人敦促观众向在冠状病毒大流行中受苦的人致敬。
 我们正在努力建立一个更温和，更友善的社会，如果我们全都投入一点，我们将要到达那里，＆＃34;特雷贝克说。
 他的最后几场演出原定于圣诞节一周播出，但推迟到本周才放出，以使更多人获得曝光。
    在Trebek的8200多个剧集中向参赛者传递的50万条线索中，最后一条是他说的： 
＆＃34;基本上，这部令人心动的弗兰克·卡普拉（Frank Capra）的经典作品是关于一位金融家的前夕经历极为艰难的失落的金融家。  特雷贝克显然不确定星期五的一集将是他录制时的最后一集。  该节目的执行制片人Mike Richards告诉NBC的今天。 周一表示Trebek是绝对的战士。 尽管处于巨大痛苦中，但在他最后的录音会议中。  在剧集的结尾，在视频致敬之前，特雷贝克说：“下周再见。”
note_en:
More than two months after Alex Trebek's death, fans of "Jeopardy!" finally got the chance to say goodbye. A video tribute to the host closed Friday's episode of the quiz show, the final one that Trebek taped before pancreatic cancer   claimed his life on November 8.
   The 90-second montage, set to Hugh Jackman singing the Peter Allen song "Once Before I Go," is a lighthearted and laughter-filled remembrance showing Trebek's changing look through his 36 years as host, with moustache and without, with black hair and with grey, with suits from several decades.
  It celebrated the wackier moments of the usually strait-laced Trebek, showing him verbally sparring with contestants and arm-wrestling with one.
  "You really make me feel inadequate," he tells a child contestant. "Sorry about that," she sassily answers.
 Trebek is shown walking on the set pants-less in one clip, dressed as the Statue of Liberty in another, and wearing the costume of a Trojan solider in another.
 A clip of the host declaring "I don't dance" is shown amid scenes of him dancing through the years.
 The tribute is still plenty sad and sentimental, though, as Jackman sings "it's so hard to say good-byes, when there's so much that's left unspoken in your eyes," and "once before I go, I would like to let you know, that I would do it all again."
   And in its final moments it shows Trebek through signing off through the decades, repeatedly saying, "So long, everybody."
 Trebek died on November 8 at age 80, but had pre-taped several weeks of shows that have continued to air. He kept working for nearly two years after his diagnosis with pancreatic cancer, remaining in place at the podium where he had become an institution since starting in 1984.
 The show will continue next week with   a series of interim hosts, starting with veteran "Jeopardy!" champion Ken Jennings.
 The week's final Trebek episodes began Monday with the host urging viewers to give to others who were suffering during the coronavirus pandemic.
 "We're trying to build a gentler, kinder society, and if we all pitch in just a little bit, we're going to get there," Trebek said.
 His final shows were originally scheduled to air Christmas week, but they were put off until this week to give the send-off more exposure.
    In one of the last of the more than 500,000 clues Trebek delivered to contestants in his more than 8,200 episodes, he says:
 "Basically, this heartwarming Frank Capra classic is about a disgraced financier having an incredibly rough Christmas Eve."
  Trebek apparently wasn't certain that Friday's episode would be his last when it was recorded.
 The show's executive producer, Mike Richards, told NBC's "Today" show on Monday that Trebek was "an absolute warrior" in his last taping sessions, despite being in "enormous pain."
 At the end of the episode, before the video tribute, Trebek says, "see you again next week."
title: 《美国测验》节目主持人亚历克斯·特雷贝克播出的最后一集
title_en: Final Episode Aired For American Quiz Show Host Alex Trebek

note:
从数量上看，人类历史上没有人比唐纳德·J·特朗普拥有更多的阴谋论。在上周的所有重大事件中，沉默社交媒体扩音器不仅是美国政治的“巨大”时刻，而且是从公共卫生到国家安全等一系列问题。
 在研究LikeWar，Emerson Brooking的书以及我关于社交媒体武器化的书时，我实际上回过头来阅读了每条@realdonaldtrump推文，回到了他的第一篇推文：2009年5月4日，宣布他即将在莱特曼秀上露面。当您浏览随后的57,000多个推文时，谎言和侮辱的巨大规模令人发麻。然而，什么也显着（我对我的“信息战创伤后应激障碍。”笑话）是许多阴谋论特鲁姆普怎么都启动或大规模就任总统不久升高。它们的范围从众所周知的谎言（如出生主义）到其他在回顾中甚至更卑鄙的谎言（如煽动反疫苗神话）。
 最重要的是，我们发现特朗普在说服他人传播他的阴谋论方面非常有效。我们的研究表明，就像在公共卫生中一样，超级传播者是病毒传播的关键。降低互联网毒性的途径是限制这些超级传播者，无论他们是ISIS宣传者还是右翼极端主义者。与其试图监管所有人，不如将注意力集中在影响所有人的关键节点上。
 禁止特朗普显然是社交媒体的头条新闻，但它反映了创建和运营这些如今必不可少的网络的公司的更大政策转变。这些公司现在越来越多地根据用户或帖子是否违反规则来做出内容审核决定，而且这些决定可能会对网络外的人产生什么影响。随着公司进行调整以减少COVID-19错误信息，这种情况已经发生了转变，但在特朗普被驱逐后达到了顶点。
 在过去的一年中，在对国会大厦的暴力扣押中，最具爆炸性的是，社交媒体和现实世界的政治环境都发生了变化。但是特朗普似乎并不了解。或者，也许是，从一开始就从不追究责任，即将卸任的总统认为他可以继续以同样的方式进行工作：越过一条线，然后越走越远。重要的是，Twitter决定他已经越过了最后一条线。他不仅屡次违反了该平台关于选举欺诈主张的规则。现在，即使在国会大厦举行了所有活动之后，在最初的停职之后，他仍然使用Twitter返回Twitter，立即打破了他在前一天晚上发布的一段录像中做出的“和平过渡”的承诺。
 媒体和政治领域缺少太多东西，但Twitter和其他平台无法忽略的是，特朗普宣布他将不参加就职典礼。有了这个，他不仅回到了和平过渡的承诺上，而且再次将汽油扔在了火上。计划在1月17日在各州首府举行的一系列极端民兵事件。 （美国国会大厦的风暴并没有孤立；上周看到亲特朗普的暴民也企图或成功闯入佐治亚州，内华达州，南卡罗来纳州，犹他州和华盛顿州的州立立法机构和州长的住所。）更令人担忧的是，安全分析师在网上讨论了定于1月20日在华盛顿特区举行的“百万民兵游行”的活动。其目的，至少是在聊天中，不仅是要破坏拜登的就职典礼，而且还希望为警察争取暴力回报。在国会大厦被杀的暴徒的所谓“难”。 Twitter官员得出结论，特朗普的推文“很可能会激发其他人复制2021年1月6日发生的暴力行为。”
 无论特朗普是否打算给双狗吹口哨，即使他们狂暴地高喊“ Hang Mike Pence，Hang Mike Pence”和平台，他都告诉他“喜欢”的“爱国者”都这样说。拥有他传播混乱消息所需的网络的公司。对于他们来说，就像我们其他人一样，特朗普已经失去了怀疑的好处。 
作为这一更大转变的一部分，特朗普脱胎换骨的回响不仅会在未来几天，而且会长期蔓延，从恐怖主义到公共卫生，无所不包。原因是它从根本上改变了比赛场地。
 社交媒体生态系统中的所有内容都曾经受到有毒力量的青睐，从将我们的内容提要推向极端主义的算法，到公司长期不愿接受它的算法。想象一下桌上倾斜的桌上足球游戏。是的，小足球运动员可以尝试阻止每次掷入掷球的动作，但最后的一切旋转都无关紧要。但是，在过去的几年中，该表已开始纠正。在外界对选举虚假信息，大规模杀戮和COVID-19迫在眉睫的压力（可能最重要的是内部员工起义）的驱使下，这两家公司的领导人采取了一系列措施，以使其更难产生有毒力量。从禁止某些类型的广告到消除某些谎言，这些保护措施是逐步建立起来的，最终导致互联网响亮的声音逐渐消失。
 当然，这种转变早就该了。每个新政策都是在事实发生之后提出的。我们当中从事此主题工作的人们都知道，社交媒体引发的许多可怕事件本可以避免或至少通过此类行动得到缓解。对我本人来说，匹兹堡发生大规模屠杀事件后，一位高级社交媒体高管告诉我那是最关键的时刻，尽管他的公司能够限制ISIS对他们网络的使用，但是技术和法律原因意味着他们无法采用这种方式。对极右翼极端分子采取同样的措施。但是在又发生了几起与社交媒体相关的大规模屠杀事件之后（基督城，埃尔帕索等），他所说的“不可能”突然变成了可能。正如互联网的评论所言，这两家公司的经营惯例有点像卡尔文和霍布斯漫画中的卡尔文鲍尔，其中规则是在制定过程中制定的。
 然而，似乎经常反复无常和不负责任的事情也是可以理解的。这些公司面临着迅速变化的业务，技术和战场。做出这类决定并不是大多数科技公司的人在打包行李到硅谷时打算做的事情。他们想建立并获利，而不是警察。
 那么，这一切现在离开了我们哪里呢？当然，这些公司似乎终于意识到自己承担着全新的责任。他们不仅是技术创造者，甚至还不只是新闻媒体编辑。经过多年的躲避，他们发现自己正在运行信息战区。并且有一个关键的变化来自对社交媒体不仅是交流空间而且是冲突空间的理解。用克劳塞维兹主义的术语来说，毒性的作用现在正面临一种全新的“摩擦”。
 当然，特朗普禁止从Twitter到Pinterest等平台的禁令不会结束阴谋论的传播。例如，国会议员马特·盖茨（Matt Gaetz）等超级超级传播者，在第一天就将“国会大厦”骚乱视为“反法”的谎言推向了50万以上的参与。特朗普从社交媒体流放也不会结束极端主义的风险。我们已经看到极端分子聚集在更小，更紧的网中，有些在诸如Parler或Gab之类的网络中公开存在，而另一些则不可见。与ISIS或Alex Jones一样，忠实的信徒将跟随，但会进入较小的兔子洞。
 然而，在这个新的冲突空间中，最重要的演员刚刚离开董事会。原始数据表明，特朗普不仅是关键节点，而且还是阴谋论和极端主义（从极右翼极端主义到QAnon到反vaxxers）多个重叠宇宙的字面中心。现在，每个都有一个字面上的黑洞。 
尽管大自然可能会令人厌恶，但这些将不容易被填补。 那个人并不是唯一。 最终，为控制历史上最伟大的超级传播者而采取的步骤将使追随者更难产生相同的效果。
note_en:
By the numbers, no person in human history has shared more conspiracy theories with a greater number of people than Donald J. Trump. Among all the momentous events of the last week, the  silencing of his social-media megaphones is a “yuge” moment not just for American politics but a host of issues from public health to national security.
 In researching   LikeWar, Emerson Brooking’s and my book on the weaponization of social media, I actually went back and read every single @realdonaldtrump tweet, going back to his very first: a May 4, 2009,  announcement of his upcoming appearance on the Letterman show. As you sift through the more than 57,000 tweets that follow, the sheer scale of the lies and insults becomes mind-numbing. (I joke about my “information warfare PTSD.”) Yet what is also notable is how many conspiracy theories Trump both started or massively elevated long before becoming president. They ranged from well-known lies like birtherism to other ones that are even more despicable in retrospect, like fueling anti-vaccine myths.
 Most importantly, we found that Trump was spectacularly effective in persuading others to spread his conspiracy theories. Our research showed that, just like in public health, superspreaders are the key to virality. The path to making the internet less toxic is placing limits on these superspreaders, be they ISIS propagandists or right-wing extremists. Instead of trying to police everyone, we must focus on key nodes that affect everyone.
 Banning Trump is obviously the headline event for social media, but it reflects a larger policy shift by the companies that created and run these now-essential networks. These firms are now making content moderation decisions based increasingly not just on whether a user or a post violated their rules, but what effect these might have on people off the network. This was already shifting as firms adjusted to reduce COVID-19 misinformation, but hit its culmination in Trump’s ejection.
 Over the last year, and seen most explosively in the violent seizing of the Capitol, the political context changed, both on social media and in the real world. But Trump didn’t seem to understand it. Or, maybe, having never been held accountable from birth onwards, the outgoing president thought he could keep on operating the same way: crossing a line, and getting away with it. Importantly, Twitter  decided he had crossed a final line. He had not just repeatedly broken the platform’s rules on election-fraud claims. Now, even after all the events at the Capitol, he had used his return to Twitter after an initial suspension to immediately break the pledge of a “peaceful transition” that he had made in a  stilted video released just the night before.
 What too many in media and politics are missing, but what Twitter and the other platforms couldn’t ignore, was Trump’s  announcement that he would not be participating in the Inaugural events. With that, he didn’t just go back on his pledge of peaceful transition, but threw gasoline on the fire yet again. There were already a series of  extremist militia events planned for Jan. 17 in various state capitals. (The storming of the U.S. Capitol was not isolated; last week saw armed pro-Trump mobs also attempt or succeed in  breaking into state legislatures and governors’ homes in Georgia, Nevada, South Carolina, Utah, and Washington state.) Even more worrisome, security analysts had picked up online discussion of a “ Million Militia March” set for Jan. 20 in Washington, D.C. Its purpose, at least in the chatter, is not just to disrupt Biden’s inauguration, but also to seek violent payback on police for the supposed “ martyrdom” of the rioter killed in the Capitol. Twitter officials  concluded that Trump’s tweets “are likely to inspire others to replicate the violent acts that took place on January 6, 2021.”
 Whether Trump intended the dual dog whistle or not, it was heard that way by both the “patriots” whom he’d told he “loved” even as they rampaged and chanted “Hang Mike Pence, Hang Mike Pence,” and by the platform companies that own the networks he needed for his rabble-rousing messages. And for them, as it should be for the rest of us, Trump had lost the benefit of the doubt.
 The reverberations of Trump’s deplatforming as part of this larger shift will shake out for not just the coming days, but over the long term — and in everything from terrorism to public health. The reason is that it fundamentally alters the playing field.
 Everything in the social media ecosystem was once tilted in the favor of toxic forces, from the algorithms that push our content feeds toward extremism to the companies’ longstanding reticence to admit it. Imagine a foosball game on a slanted table. Yes, the little soccer players could try to stop each rush of the rolling ball, but all their spinning wouldn’t matter in the end. Over the past few years, however, that table has started to be righted. Driven by outside pressure over election disinformation, mass killings, and COVID-19 striking close to home — and perhaps most significantly, internal employee revolts — the companies’ leaders have put into place a series of measures that make it harder for toxic forces. From banning certain types of ads to de-ranking certain lies, these safeguards built up, piece by piece, culminating in the deplatforming of the Internet’s loudest voice.
 Of course, this shift is long overdue. Each new policy came after the fact. Those of us who work on this topic know that many horrible events fueled by social media could have been avoided or at least mitigated with such actions. For myself, the crystalizing moment was being told by a senior social-media executive after the  mass killing in Pittsburgh that although his company had been able to limit ISIS’ use of their network, technical and legal reasons meant they couldn’t apply the very same measures to far-right extremists. But after several more social media-linked mass killings —  Christchurch,  El Paso, etc. — what he’d said was “impossible” suddenly became possible. As Internet wags have commented, the firms’ operating practices have been a bit like  Calvinball from the Calvin and Hobbes comic, in which the rules are made up as they go.
 Yet what often seems capricious and irresponsible is also understandable. The companies faced a rapidly changing business, technology, and battlefield. And making these kind of decisions is not what most people in tech firms planned on doing when they packed their bags for Silicon Valley. They want to build and profit, not police.
 So where does this all leave us now? It certainly seems that the firms have finally come to the realization that they have a whole new set of responsibilities. They are not just tech creators or even the equivalent of news-media editors. After years of dodging it, they get that they are running information warzones. And there is a key change that comes from understanding that social media is not just a communication space but a conflict space. In Clausewitzian terms, the forces of toxicity now face a whole new type of “friction.”
 Of course, Trump’s ban from platforms that range from Twitter to Pinterest won’t end the spread of conspiracy theories. For example, the lie that the Capitol riot was the work of antifa was pushed to over a half million engagements within its first day by lesser superspreaders like Rep. Matt Gaetz. Nor will Trump’s exile from social media end the risk of extremism. Already, we see extremists clustering in smaller, tighter nets, some out in the open on networks like Parler or Gab and some hidden from view. As with ISIS or Alex Jones, the faithful will follow, but into smaller rabbit holes.
 Yet, in this new conflict space, the most important actor just got moved off the board. The raw numbers show that Trump is not just a key node, but the literal center of multiple overlapping universes of conspiracy theory and extremism, from far-right extremism to QAnon to anti-vaxxers. Now, there is a literal black hole in each.
 And while nature may abhor a vacuum, these will not be easily filled. It isn’t that the man is unique. It is that the steps finally taken to rein in history’s greatest superspreader will made it harder for those who follow to have the same effect.
title: 防御一

note:
最近泄漏的有关突破性收听项目中检测到的有趣的，潜在的外星无线电信号的消息可能未必是“它”，即我们银河系中某个技术物种的明确标志，但仍然提供了一个巨大的机会对宇宙生命本质的一些反思。
  已经报道了这种奇怪的窄带嗡嗡声的一些细节，频率大约为982.002 MHz，并且明显与半人马的Proxima方向一致，我们将需要等待一会儿才能进行完整的技术分析。在没有任何进一步的见解的情况下，科学家目前可以说的最好的就是该信号引起了极大的兴趣，但是我们必须假定一个解释很可能是世俗的（或者至少在公认的已知现象的万神殿之内）。 ），而不是外星情报和代理机构的标志。
  尽管如此，在听到这个消息时，有人想知道，当（以及是否）我们最终找到证据证明我们并不孤单时，这会是什么样子。毕竟，2020年是一年的沉闷。人类时间轴上一个奇怪而令人恐惧的分支，人们常常觉得这是另一种现实，我们最好避免。为什么不将其他技术生命的发现添加到混合中呢？因此，为什么这个发现不应该以相对普通的方式悄悄出现在我们身上？
  好莱坞的初次接触效果图，或者实际上是为向世界宣布确实有外星人而存在的（并且已经讨论了很长时间的）概念协议，我们往往对此很感兴趣。但是，对于所有这些倾向和计划，故事都可能像这样容易发生：谣言，新闻泄漏，科学家的初步讨论，然后发现并基本上已经结束，事实人口稠密的星系刚刚成为另一历史：都是由于来自Proxima Centauri的单调载波信号源自一些普通的外星活动。也许只是他们适度的行星际飞船的遥测波段，或者某种行星雷达，或者刚起步的行星界通信系统，或者谁知道。没有花哨的数据流或有目的的信号，只是一个物种正以我们的方式进行业务。
  我们发现其他恒星周围的行星也有一个有趣的相似之处。早在1990年代初，我们就获得了脉冲星周围行星大小物体的第一个证据。一项令人惊讶且完全出乎意料的发现，但我们也许没有像我们所能想象的那样高兴很多，因为它根本不像“正常”行星系统（对我们而言，以地球为中心的思想家）。然后，第一个被发现的围绕日照恒星的行星也是在紧凑轨道上的一类意料之外的巨型世界。最终，再过大约十年后，很明显，所有条纹的丰富行星都是正常现象，而不是例外情况。今天，很难想象我们曾经真的以为可能会这样。如今行星稀少的宇宙似乎显得荒唐。
  也许这也是寻找外星情报的方式。最初会有一些奇怪之处，有些好奇之处并非我们计划的。例如，沉闷的载波信号。随着时间的流逝，将会出现更多的证据，直到最终人们清楚地知道那里有很多物种，在自己的小社区里四处闲逛，没有做任何真正非凡的事情，因为最终，这些可能性更多地是我们生动的想象力的产物比宇宙强迫生命走向的任何事物都重要。
  当然，我有点face，在宇宙中其他地方首次发现任何种类的生命将是令人震惊的和改变世界的事情，而技术生命将排在冲击表的最顶端。但是震惊过去了，我们也无法确切知道这将如何发挥作用。不管发生什么事，谣言和初步调查结果都会使您感到意外。 
最终，这可能只是一种缓解。 我们既不会孤单，也不会被任何特别的事物所包围。 哥白尼的平庸性将有所恢复，我们可以再担心在岩石和水流过宇宙的过程中可能会出错的所有其他问题。
note_en:
The recently leaked news about an intriguing, potentially extraterrestrial radio signal detected as part of the  Breakthrough Listen project may not turn out to be “it”—the unequivocal sign of a technological species out there in our galaxy—but still offers a great opportunity for some reflection on the nature of cosmic life.
  Some details of this curious narrowband hum at a frequency of around 982.002 MHz, and its apparent coincidence with the direction of Proxima Centauri, have  been reported, and we’ll have to wait a little while longer for the full technical analysis to be presented. In the absence of any further insights the best that scientists can say for now is that this signal is of great interest, but we must assume that an explanation is much more likely to be mundane (or at least within the pantheon of recognizable, known phenomena) than a sign of extraterrestrial intelligence and agency.
  Nonetheless, in hearing this news, one wonders whether this is what it will feel like when (and if) we eventually find evidence that we’re not alone in the cosmos. After all, 2020 has been a doozy of a year; a weird, horrifying branch of the human timeline that has so often felt like it was an alternate reality we would’ve done better to avoid. Why not add the discovery of other technological life to the mix? For that matter, why shouldn’t that discovery just sneak up on us in a comparatively ordinary fashion?
  We tend to be well primed by Hollywood renderings of first contact, or indeed the  notional protocols that are in place (and that have been long discussed) for announcing to the world that there are indeed aliens out there. But for all those predispositions and plans the story could just as easily happen like this: a rumor, a leaked bit of news, a preliminary discussion from the scientists, and then lo-and-behold it’s basically all over, and the fact of a populated galaxy just becomes another piece of history: All because of a monotone carrier wave signal from Proxima Centauri emanating from some ordinary alien activity. Perhaps just a telemetry band for their modest interplanetary spacecraft, or some kind of planetary radar, or a fledgling planet-bound communication system, or who knows what. No fancy data stream or purposefully directed signal, just a species going about its business in precisely the way that we do.
  There’s an interesting parallel too with our discovery of planets around other stars. Back in the early 1990s we had the first evidence of planet-sized objects  around pulsars. An astonishing and wholly unexpected discovery, but one that we perhaps didn’t quite rejoice in as much as we could have because it just wasn’t anything like a “normal” planetary system (for us Earth-centric thinkers). Then, the first detected planets around sunlike stars were also a class of unanticipated giant worlds on compact orbits. Eventually, after another decade or so, it became apparent that abundant planets of all stripes are the norm rather than the exception. Today it’s hard to imagine that we ever really thought it could be otherwise; a cosmos where planets would be rare now seems rather absurd.
  Perhaps that is how it will also go for the search for extraterrestrial intelligence. There’ll be some initial oddities, some curiosities that aren’t quite the things we planned for. A dull carrier wave signal for instance. Over time more evidence will show up, until eventually it’s clear that there are lots of species out there, puttering around in their own little neighborhoods and doing nothing truly extraordinary, because those possibilities were, in the end, more the product of our lively imaginations than anything that the universe compels life towards.
  Of course, I’m being a little facetious, the first discovery of life of any kind elsewhere in the universe would be shocking and world-changing, and technological life would rank at the very top of the shock-o-meter. But shock passes, and we also have no way of knowing exactly how this would play out. Rumors and preliminary findings have a way of dulling surprises, no matter what’s at stake.
  Eventually it might all just be a bit of a relief. We’ll neither be alone, nor surrounded by anything particularly extraordinary. Copernican mediocrity will be somewhat restored, and we can go back to worrying about everything else that can go wrong on our speck of rock and water as it sails through the cosmos.
title: 无聊的外星人

气象学家说，极地涡旋是一团急速旋转的寒冷空气，高出北极上方，正在发生怪异的颤动，可能很快将给美国东部，北欧和东亚带来寒冷和多雪的天气，持续数周。
  Atmospheric季节性预报主任Judah Cohen说，虽然极地涡旋上升并不少见，但这种特殊的重新配置-徘徊并可能分裂为两个-可能与迅速变暖的北极地区的气候变化有关。以及马萨诸塞州的环境研究公司，该公司是风险评估公司Verisk Analytics的一部分。
  ＆＃34;预计美国东部地区的冬天比上半年的冬天要多，＆＃34;科恩告诉Live Science。
   北极的升温速度比世界上任何其他地区都要快。结果，那里的海冰覆盖面积在缩小。根据《国家雪》和《国家雪景》，到2020年9月和2020年12月，北极海冰覆盖率分别缩减至有记录以来的第二低和第三低的最低水平。冰数据中心。
  科恩说，北极地区的气温比平常高，这可能使极地涡旋消失。极地涡旋是一个大范围的低压区，位于平流层中的北极上方，即对流层上方的对流层，对流层是地球上大气层最低的层，是大多数天气条件发生的最低层。这种低压系统通常充满冷的回旋空气。在冬季，使极地涡旋保持在适当位置的喷气流有时会减弱，从而使涡旋的冷空气向南延伸。
    科恩及其同事建议，北极海冰的覆盖面越少，则意味着越多的海潮从正常的西伯利亚干燥大陆向内陆迁移。然后，这些水分变成雪，雪将热量反射回太空，使西伯利亚比平时更冷。反过来破坏了延伸到欧亚大陆对流层的热带。 Cohen和他的同事在2019年的《自然气候变化》（Nature Climate Change）评论中写道，这个散乱的带会破坏极地涡旋的稳定性，从而导致美国洛矶山脉以东，北欧和东亚的冬天更加寒冷。
 ＆＃34;像极地漩涡一样旋转着安静，快速旋转的陀螺，＆＃34;科恩说。 ＆＃34;然后，您有[来自对流层]的能量开始爆炸在旋转的极地涡旋上，使其摇摇晃晃。 
他补充说，这个季节迄今为止，西伯利亚的降雪量已经超过正常水平。因此，我确实相信它导致了弱极性涡旋。
  并非每个人都同意这种西伯利亚雪地和摆动的极地涡旋联系增加，但是很明显，极地涡旋减弱会导致北半球某些地区的冬天更冷。人们还接受了所谓的平流层突然变暖（SSW）事件会减弱极地涡旋并使它摇摇欲坠。当与天气系统相关的大规模大气波进入平流层并破坏极地涡旋，使其在几天之内变慢并加热至高达90华氏度（50摄氏度）时，就会发生SSW。
 Cohen指出，与北极消失的海冰有关的天气条件可能触发SSW。 《华盛顿邮报》报道，SSW平均每10年发生6次，而现在我们正经历着巨大的SSW。
 科罗拉多州博尔德市国家海洋与大气管理局化学科学实验室的研究科学家艾米·巴特勒（Amy Butler）表示，SSW可能是由高压，低压系统引起的。
 在过去的几周中，北大西洋大部分地区和北欧/亚洲持续存在高压系统，而北太平洋则存在低压系统，＆＃34;巴特勒在一封电子邮件中告诉Live Science。众所周知，这种高压，低压二重奏会破坏极涡旋所在的平流层。
 几天前在北太平洋发生的极端炸弹旋风（迅速形成的冬季风暴，具有飓风强度的风）也可能促成了南太平洋海啸，但必须对此进行调查。进一步，＆＃34;她说。
  1月5日，极地旋涡的逆时针风向反转（这表明发生了突然的大气变暖事件），并且该旋涡从通常的位置绕北极移居，向欧洲和北大西洋方向移动。 ，巴特勒说。科恩说，在这段时间里，它开始分裂（但没有完全分裂）。 
极地涡旋可能会在10天内进一步分裂，但目前尚不清楚这是否会发生，巴特勒说。 ＆＃34;预测模型难以在一个多星期前就预测涡旋的分裂。
  极地涡旋的破坏是预报的关键，因为对流爆发大约在两周后，对流层出现了奇怪的天气，可能持续数周。由于本周的极地涡旋中断，有迹象表明我们将在两周内看到天气转冷……在美国东部，北欧和东亚，＆＃34;科恩说。
 他说，就目前而言，这是否意味着暴风雪还是一阵冷空气在空中。
 同时，在加拿大北极和亚热带的亚洲和非洲，也可能出现比正常情况更暖的情况。巴特勒说。 ＆＃34;平流层突然变暖后，这些影响可能会持续4-6周。

note_en:
High above the North Pole, the   polar vortex, a fast-spinning whirl of frigid air, is doing a weird shimmy that may soon bring cold and snowy weather to the Eastern U.S., Northern Europe and East Asia for weeks on end, meteorologists say.
  While it's not unusual for the polar vortex to act up, this particular reconfiguration — wandering around and possibly splitting in two — may be tied to   climate change in the rapidly warming Arctic, said Judah Cohen, director of seasonal forecasting at Atmospheric and Environmental Research in Massachusetts, part of Verisk Analytics, a risk-assessment company.
  "Expect a more wintery back-half of winter here in the Eastern U.S. than what we had in the first half," Cohen told Live Science.
   The   Arctic is heating up faster than any other region in the world. As a result, sea-ice cover there is shrinking — in   September 2020 and December 2020, the Arctic sea-ice cover shrunk to its second-lowest and third-lowest minimum on record for those months, respectively,   according to the National Snow and Ice Data Center.
  The warmer-than-usual temperatures in the Arctic are likely throwing the polar vortex out of whack, Cohen said. The polar vortex is a   vast area of low pressure that sits high above the Arctic in the stratosphere — the layer above the troposphere, the lowest layer of Earth's atmosphere where most weather conditions happen. This low-pressure system is usually filled with cold, swirling air. During the winter, a jet stream of air that keeps the polar vortex in place sometimes weakens, allowing the vortex's chilly air to extend southward.
    Cohen and colleagues have suggested that less Arctic sea-ice cover means there's more moisture from the sea migrating inland over normally dry Siberia. This moisture then turns into snow, which reflects heat back into space and is making Siberia colder than normal; that in turn disrupts a thermal band in the troposphere extending over Eurasia. This discombobulated band can then destabilize the polar vortex, causing colder winters east of the Rockies in the U.S. and in Northern Europe and East Asia, Cohen and his colleagues wrote in a 2019 review in the journal   Nature Climate Change.
 "Think of the polar vortex like a quiet, fast spinning top that spins in place," Cohen said. "Then, you have this energy [from the troposphere] that starts banging" on the spinning polar vortex, making it wobble and wander.
 He added that this season, "snowfall across Siberia has been above normal so far. Therefore, I do believe it has contributed to the weak polar vortex."
  Not everyone agrees with this increased-Siberian-snow-and-wobbly-polar-vortex connection, but it is clear that a weakened polar vortex  leads to colder winters in certain parts of the Northern Hemisphere. It's also accepted that so-called sudden stratospheric warming (SSW) events can weaken the polar vortex and make it teeter around. SSWs happen when large-scale atmospheric waves associated with weather systems reach into the stratosphere and disrupt the polar vortex, causing it to slow down and  heat up as much as 90 degrees Fahrenheit (50 degrees Celsius) within a few days.
 Cohen noted that SSWs can be triggered by weather conditions associated with the Arctic's disappearing sea ice. SSWs happen an average of six times every 10 years, and right now we're experiencing a big SSW,  The Washington Post reported.
 It's possible the SSW was caused by a high-pressure, low-pressure system, said Amy Butler, a research scientist at the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration Chemical Sciences Laboratory in Boulder, Colorado.
 "Over the last few weeks, there was a persistent high-pressure system over much of the North Atlantic and northern Europe/Asia, and a low-pressure system over the North Pacific," Butler told Live Science in an email. This high-pressure, low-pressure duo is known to disrupt the stratosphere, where the polar vortex lives.
 It's also possible that the extreme   bomb cyclone (a rapidly-forming winter storm with hurricane-strength winds) in the North Pacific a few days ago, contributed to the SSW, "but that will have to be investigated further," she said.
  On Jan. 5, the polar vortex's counter-clockwise winds reversed direction (a clue that a sudden atmospheric warming event had happened) and the vortex wandered from its usual location centered over the North Pole, toward Europe and the North Atlantic, Butler said. During that time, it began to (but didn't completely) split, Cohen said.
 The polar vortex might split further in about 10 days, "but it's unclear if this will happen," Butler said. "Forecast models struggle with predicting a splitting of the vortex more than a week in advance."
  Disruptions to the polar vortex are key for forecasts, as about two weeks after they happen, the troposphere gets a wallop of weird weather, which can last for weeks. Because of this week's polar vortex disruption, "there's indications we'll see some colder weather within two weeks … in the Eastern U.S., Northern Europe and East Asia," Cohen said.
 For now, it's up in the air whether that means snowstorms or a rash of cold air, he said.
 Meanwhile, "warmer-than-normal conditions can also occur over the Canadian Arctic and subtropical Asia and Africa," Butler said. "These effects could potentially persist for 4-6 weeks after the sudden stratospheric warming."

  国家情报局局长和国防部长现在有不到六个月的时间向国会情报和武装部队委员会提供有关“未识别的空中现象”的未分类报告。
 这是一项被纳入委员会评论的规定。 《 2021财年情报授权法》中的这一部分，包含在庞大的支出法案中。
  根据参议院情报委员会的指示，该报告必须包含由海军情报局，不明航空现象工作队和FBI收集的UFO数据和情报的详细分析。
  它还应详细描述跨机构流程，以确保及时收集数据并集中分析联邦政府报告的所有未识别的空中现象。并指定负责该程序的官员。
 最后，委员会说，报告应确定不明飞行物所构成的任何潜在的国家安全威胁，并评估该国的任何对手是否可能是这种活动的幕后黑手。
 委员会说，提交的报告应该是保密的，尽管它可以包含保密的附件。 
国家情报局局长办公室发言人向事实检查网站Snopes证实了这一消息。
  五角大楼在去年四月发布了三段短视频，显示了“不明航空现象”。 -美国海军先前确认的剪辑是真实的。
 这段录像来自2004年，另一段来自2015年，显示了在用红外热像仪录制时正在快速移动的未识别飞行物体。其中两个视频包含服务成员对物体移动的速度感到敬畏的反应。一个声音推测它可能是无人机。
  仍然不清楚这些对象是什么，并且关于它们的来源还没有共识。一些人认为，它们可能是试图收集情报的地上敌人所操纵的无人机，而不是我们通常等同于不明飞行物的外星人。
  国会议员和五角大楼的官员们长期以来一直担心在美国军事基地上空飞行的不明飞机的外观。参议院情报委员会去年6月投票通过，要求五角大楼和情报界对遭遇事件进行公开分析。
 但这并不是五角大楼第一次调查与未知物体的空中相遇。五角大楼以前是根据前参议员哈里·里德（Harry Reid）的要求启动的一项自密闭机密计划的一部分，研究了此类事件的记录。
 五角大楼表示，该计划于2007年启动，并于2012年结束，因为他们评估了还有更高的优先事项需要资金。

(CNN)When President Donald Trump  signed the $2.3 trillion coronavirus relief and government funding bill into law in December, so began the 180-day countdown for US intelligence agencies to tell Congress  what they know about UFOs.
  The director of National Intelligence and the secretary of defense have a little less than six months now to provide the congressional intelligence and armed services committees with an unclassified report about "unidentified aerial phenomena."
 It's a stipulation that was tucked into the "committee comment" section of the  Intelligence Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2021, which was contained in the massive spending bill.
  That report must contain detailed analyses of UFO data and intelligence collected by the Office of Naval Intelligence, the  Unidentified Aerial Phenomena Task Force and the FBI, according to the Senate intelligence committee's directive.
  It should also describe in detail "an interagency process for ensuring timely data collection and centralized analysis of all unidentified aerial phenomena reporting for the Federal Government" and designate an official responsible for that process.
 Finally, the report should identify any potential national security threats posed by UFOs and assess whether any of the nation's adversaries could be behind such activity, the committee said.
 The submitted report should be unclassified, the committee said, though it can contain a classified annex.
 A spokesperson for the Office of the Director of National Intelligence confirmed the news to the fact-checking website  Snopes.
  The Pentagon  released three short videos in April of last year showing "unidentified aerial phenomena" -- clips that the US Navy had  previously confirmed were real.
 The videos, one from 2004 and the other two from 2015, show what appear to be unidentified flying objects rapidly moving while recorded by infrared cameras. Two of the videos contain service members reacting in awe at how quickly the objects are moving. One voice speculates that it could be a drone.
  It's still unclear what the objects are, and there's no consensus on their origin. Some believe they may be drones potentially operated by earthly adversaries seeking to gather intelligence, rather than the extraterrestrials we normally equate with UFOs.
  Members of Congress and Pentagon officials have long been concerned about the appearance of the unidentified aircraft that have flown over US military bases. The Senate Intelligence Committee voted last June to have the Pentagon and intelligence community provide a public analysis of the encounters.
 But it's not the first time the Pentagon has looked into aerial encounters with unknown objects. The Pentagon  previously studied recordings of such incidents as part of a since-shuttered classified program launched at the behest of former Sen. Harry Reid.
 That program was launched in 2007 and ended in 2012, according to the Pentagon, because they assessed that there were higher priorities that needed funding.
 The former head of the program  Luis Elizondo told CNN in 2017 that he personally believes "there is very compelling evidence that we may not be alone."
